content.text: Ownership Boundaries When Claude Features Span Multiple Services

A single Claude application rarely lives in a single repository. Mature deployments split the AI layer across multiple services: a triage service, a summarisation service, a shared tool schema library, an evaluation harness. Ownership boundaries become ambiguous when a prompt change in one service affects downstream services that consume its output format, or when a shared tool schema is changed by one team but relied on by three others.

Ownership boundaries in Claude microservices answer two questions: who reviews changes to this file, and who is accountable when something goes wrong? Those two questions do not always have the same answer — the team that owns a prompt for review purposes may not be the team that gets paged when that prompt causes an incident.

This sub-subtopic defines ownership boundary patterns for monorepos and polyrepos, how to propagate mandatory @ai-safety-team and @api-team ownership across repositories, the shared-schema ownership problem and its solutions, and how to write tooling that validates that cross-repo ownership contracts are honoured.
Monorepo vs. Polyrepo Ownership Patterns

attrs.alt: Monorepo (single CODEOWNERS) vs. polyrepo (per-repo CODEOWNERS) ownership patterns

content.text: The two dominant repository structures each have different ownership challenges:
Structure
Ownership Mechanism
Key Challenge
Recommended Pattern
Monorepo
Single .github/CODEOWNERS file using service-path prefixes
Rules become long and are hard to audit when the service count grows past ~5
Prefix all rules with the service path; use a coverage audit script to detect gaps when new services are added
Polyrepo
One CODEOWNERS per repo; cross-repo ownership is implicit
No single place to enforce that every repo containing prompts/ is owned by @ai-safety-team
Org-level policy (GitHub org rulesets) + a cross-repo audit script that checks every repo for the required patterns
Monorepo: Service-Prefixed Ownership Rules
In a monorepo, each service directory has its own ownership block. The service team owns general code; the AI safety team is added as a co-owner on the service's prompt files:

# .github/CODEOWNERS — monorepo with two Claude services

# --- Global catch-all ---
*  @platform-team

# --- triage-service ---
triage-service/                         @triage-team
triage-service/prompts/                 @ai-safety-team
triage-service/prompts/**               @ai-safety-team
triage-service/tools/                   @api-team
triage-service/tools/**                 @api-team
triage-service/evals/                   @eval-team

# --- summariser-service ---
summariser-service/                     @summariser-team
summariser-service/prompts/             @ai-safety-team
summariser-service/prompts/**           @ai-safety-team
summariser-service/tools/               @api-team
summariser-service/tools/**             @api-team
summariser-service/evals/               @eval-team

# --- shared library ---
shared/tool-schema/                     @api-team
shared/tool-schema/**/*.json            @api-team
shared/evals/                           @eval-team
shared/evals/**                         @eval-team

# --- Infrastructure ---
.github/                                @platform-team
.github/CODEOWNERS                      @ai-safety-team @platform-team
docs/behaviour-spec.md                  @ai-safety-team @product-owners

Key design decision: the service team (@triage-team) is listed on the service root, and @ai-safety-team is listed on the prompts/ subdirectory. GitHub uses the last matching rule, so a PR that touches triage-service/prompts/system_prompt.json will require both @triage-team (for the service root) and @ai-safety-team (for the prompts path). In practice, requiring both creates too many review gates — configure branch protection to require only code-owner reviews, not all listed owners. The @ai-safety-team requirement is the critical one for prompt files.
Polyrepo: Per-Repo CODEOWNERS with a Consistent Contract

In a polyrepo, every repository that contains AI layer files must define the same ownership contract: @ai-safety-team on prompts/, @api-team on tools/. The team that owns the service's general code is the catch-all.
Service Repo (e.g., triage-service)

# triage-service/.github/CODEOWNERS

# Service team owns everything not explicitly covered.
*  @triage-team

# Prompt files — ai-safety-team required for all prompt changes.
prompts/            @ai-safety-team
prompts/**          @ai-safety-team

# Tool schemas defined in this service — api-team required.
tools/              @api-team
tools/**/*.json     @api-team

# Evaluation suite for triage — eval-team owns the quality bar.
evals/              @eval-team
evals/**            @eval-team

# Release and CI infrastructure.
.github/            @platform-team
.github/CODEOWNERS  @ai-safety-team @platform-team
Shared Tool Schema Repo

The shared tool schema repository is a special case: it has no service team, and every change to it potentially affects all consuming services. Both @api-team and @ai-safety-team must approve changes:

# shared-tool-schema/.github/CODEOWNERS

# All schema files require api-team approval.
*               @api-team
*.json          @api-team

# Breaking schema changes additionally require ai-safety-team.
# This cannot be enforced by CODEOWNERS alone — use a CI check
# that requires @ai-safety-team review when the diff contains
# a removed field or a type change.
# The pattern below adds them as co-owners on the entire repo
# as an approximation.
/               @api-team @ai-safety-team

# Evaluation suite for shared schemas.
evals/          @eval-team
evals/**        @eval-team
The Shared Schema Ownership Problem

When a tool schema is used by multiple services, changing it without coordinating with all consumers breaks those services. CODEOWNERS can require @api-team to review every schema change, but it cannot verify that the consumer services were notified or that their prompts were updated to match the new schema.

The solution is a schema dependency manifest: a file that lists which services consume which schemas, maintained by the API team. A CI job reads this manifest and comments on schema-change PRs with the list of affected services:

# scripts/check_schema_consumers.py
"""
When a shared tool schema file is changed in a PR, identify which
service repos consume that schema and post a summary to stdout.
Used in CI to ensure the PR author notifies consuming services.

Usage:
    python scripts/check_schema_consumers.py <base_sha> <head_sha>

Reads schema_consumers.json from the repo root.
"""
import subprocess, json, re, sys
from pathlib import Path

def load_consumer_manifest(manifest_path: Path) -> dict:
    """
    Load schema_consumers.json which maps schema file paths to
    a list of consuming service repo names.

    Format:
    {
      "tools/citation_tool.json": ["triage-service", "summariser-service"],
      "tools/search_tool.json":   ["triage-service"]
    }
    """
    with manifest_path.open() as f:
        return json.load(f)

def get_changed_schema_files(base: str, head: str) -> list[str]:
    """Return list of changed files in the tools/ directory."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "diff", "--name-only", f"{base}..{head}"],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
    )
    return [f for f in result.stdout.strip().splitlines()
            if f.startswith("tools/") and f.endswith(".json")]

def report_affected_consumers(base: str, head: str) -> None:
    """
    Print a summary of which services are affected by schema changes
    in the given commit range.
    """
    repo_root = Path(
        subprocess.run(
            ["git", "rev-parse", "--show-toplevel"],
            capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
        ).stdout.strip()
    )
    manifest_path = repo_root / "schema_consumers.json"
    if not manifest_path.exists():
        print("WARNING: schema_consumers.json not found — cannot identify affected services")
        return

    manifest = load_consumer_manifest(manifest_path)
    changed_schemas = get_changed_schema_files(base, head)

    if not changed_schemas:
        print("No schema files changed in this PR.")
        return

    affected: dict[str, list[str]] = {}
    for schema_path in changed_schemas:
        consumers = manifest.get(schema_path, [])
        if consumers:
            affected[schema_path] = consumers
        else:
            print(f"INFO: {schema_path} has no registered consumers in manifest")

    if not affected:
        print("No registered consumers affected by the changed schemas.")
        return

    print("\n=== Schema Consumer Impact Report ===")
    for schema, consumers in affected.items():
        print(f"\n{schema} is consumed by:")
        for svc in consumers:
            print(f"  - {svc}")
    print("\nACTION REQUIRED: Notify the above service teams before merging.")
    print("If this change is breaking (removed field, type change), open")
    print("a coordinated migration issue before this PR is merged.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    base_sha, head_sha = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2]
    report_affected_consumers(base_sha, head_sha)

# In the shared-tool-schema repo CI
- name: Check schema consumers
  run: |
    python scripts/check_schema_consumers.py \
      "${{ github.event.pull_request.base.sha }}" \
      "${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}"

The consumer manifest is maintained by the API team and updated whenever a new service starts consuming a shared schema. It is itself owned by @api-team in CODEOWNERS, so any change to the manifest requires API team review.
Cross-Repo Ownership Audit

In a polyrepo, there is no single file to audit. A cross-repo audit script uses the GitHub API to read the CODEOWNERS file from every repository that is known to carry prompt or tool files, and verifies that the required ownership rules are present:

# scripts/audit_cross_repo_ownership.py
"""
Audit CODEOWNERS files across all Claude service repositories to
verify that the standard ownership contract is in place.

Required contract:
  - prompts/ or prompts/** must be owned by @ai-safety-team
  - tools/ or tools/** must be owned by @api-team

Usage:
    python scripts/audit_cross_repo_ownership.py

Reads SERVICE_REPOS from the environment or a config file.
Exits with code 1 if any repo fails the contract check.
"""
import re, sys, json
import urllib.request
from pathlib import Path

# List of repos to audit.
# In practice, read from a config file or CI environment variable.
SERVICE_REPOS = [
    "example-org/triage-service",
    "example-org/summariser-service",
    "example-org/classifier-service",
]

REQUIRED_PATTERNS = {
    "@ai-safety-team": [r"^prompts/", r"^prompts/\*\*"],
    "@api-team":        [r"^tools/",   r"^tools/\*\*"],
}

def fetch_codeowners(repo: str, token: str) -> str | None:
    """
    Fetch the raw content of .github/CODEOWNERS from the given repo.
    Returns None if the file does not exist.
    """
    url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/contents/.github/CODEOWNERS"
    req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers={
        "Authorization": f"token {token}",
        "Accept": "application/vnd.github.raw",
    })
    try:
        with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as resp:
            return resp.read().decode()
    except urllib.error.HTTPError as e:
        if e.code == 404:
            return None
        raise

def check_ownership_contract(repo: str, codeowners_content: str) -> list[str]:
    """
    Verify that the required ownership patterns are present in the
    CODEOWNERS file. Returns a list of failure messages (empty = pass).
    """
    failures = []
    for team, required_patterns in REQUIRED_PATTERNS.items():
        # The contract is satisfied if at least ONE of the required
        # patterns for this team appears in the CODEOWNERS file.
        found = False
        for line in codeowners_content.splitlines():
            line = line.strip()
            if line.startswith("#") or not line:
                continue
            parts = line.split()
            path_pattern, owners = parts[0], parts[1:]
            if team in owners:
                for req in required_patterns:
                    if re.match(req, path_pattern):
                        found = True
                        break
            if found:
                break
        if not found:
            failures.append(
                f"{team} not found on any prompts/ or tools/ pattern"
            )
    return failures

def audit_all(token: str) -> bool:
    """
    Audit all service repos. Returns True if all pass, False if any fail.
    """
    all_passed = True
    for repo in SERVICE_REPOS:
        content = fetch_codeowners(repo, token)
        if content is None:
            print(f"FAIL {repo}: no .github/CODEOWNERS file found")
            all_passed = False
            continue
        failures = check_ownership_contract(repo, content)
        if failures:
            for f in failures:
                print(f"FAIL {repo}: {f}")
            all_passed = False
        else:
            print(f"OK   {repo}: ownership contract satisfied")
    return all_passed

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import os
    token = os.environ.get("GITHUB_TOKEN", "")
    if not token:
        print("ERROR: GITHUB_TOKEN not set", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    if not audit_all(token):
        sys.exit(1)
On-Call vs. Review Ownership

CODEOWNERS defines who reviews a change before it merges. On-call ownership — who gets paged when a service misbehaves in production — is a separate concept that is documented in a different file and managed by a different system (PagerDuty, OpsGenie, etc.).
For Claude services, both ownership types must be defined explicitly:
Ownership Type
Definition Location
Scope
Who Is Listed
Review ownership
.github/CODEOWNERS
Per-file/directory within a repo
@ai-safety-team for prompts, @api-team for tools, service team for general code
On-call ownership
Service metadata (PagerDuty service config, or a OWNERS.yaml at repo root)
Per-service / per-incident category
Individual engineers on the on-call rotation, not teams
Incident command
Runbook / IR process
Per-incident severity
On-call lead + escalation contacts for AI safety incidents

A common mistake is listing team names in the on-call system and individual names in CODEOWNERS — the opposite of the recommended pattern. CODEOWNERS should list teams (so membership changes do not require CODEOWNERS updates); on-call systems should list individuals (teams cannot be paged).
Summary

Ownership boundaries in Claude microservices require deliberate design: who reviews prompt changes across all services, who owns shared tool schemas, and how cross-service schema dependencies are tracked. In a monorepo, a single CODEOWNERS file with service-path prefixes covers all ownership; in a polyrepo, each repo carries its own CODEOWNERS with a consistent contract enforced by a cross-repo audit script. The shared-schema ownership problem — where a schema change in one repo affects consuming services in others — requires a consumer manifest and a CI job that reports affected services on every schema-change PR. On-call ownership and review ownership are separate concerns that belong in separate systems.
